data.title: What opportunities does OpenClaw present, and how long is the window of opportunity?
data.description:
In February 2026, Palo Alto Networks issued a security warning, pointing out that OpenClaw posed a serious prompt injection risk. That same week, Microsoft Defender recommended running OpenClaw only in completely isolated environments. Gartner directly classified it as an unacceptable cybersecurity risk. Meanwhile, OpenClaw's GitHub stars skyrocketed from 85,000 to 245,000, surpassing Linux to become the number one open-source project in GitHub history. This stark contrast itself signals that users' demand for Autonomous Agents is not only real but also urgent enough to warrant the trade-off of privacy and security. However, "validating the demand" is only the starting point. What truly determines who wins are several more core questions: What are the real challenges to Agent security? Where are the key factors in Agent competition? What do the barriers to entry look like? What's worth investing in? This article discusses our thoughts. The "secure version of OpenClaw" is unlikely to appear. Among the various expectations surrounding OpenClaw, the most common is that security issues will eventually be resolved, and the market is waiting for a "secure version of OpenClaw." However, this expectation is likely to be disappointed. The problem lies not in the engineering layer, but in the architecture of the Autonomous Agent itself. The "fatal triangle" is a consensus in the security industry. A truly autonomous agent must simultaneously meet three conditions: access to private data (emails, files, credentials), exposure to untrusted content (web pages, unfamiliar messages, third-party skills), and the ability to communicate externally (sending emails, calling APIs, publishing content). With all three conditions present, the agent is inherently vulnerable and insecure at the design level. However, removing any one of them renders the agent no longer autonomous—it cannot access private data, cannot personalize; it cannot be exposed to external content, cannot obtain new information; and it cannot communicate externally, cannot perform tasks on your behalf. The conflict between agent capabilities and security is not a trade-off, but an inherent one. This isn't theoretical deduction; Palo Alto Networks has discovered that OpenClaw's persistent memory is being exploited in this way: malicious commands can be fragmented and implanted into the agent's memory, accumulating gradually across multiple conversations and only erupting when certain conditions are met. Each fragment, viewed individually, is legitimate input. This is a completely new category of threat: memory poisoning. Traditional patches are ineffective against this attack—because it doesn't resemble an attack at all. Therefore, the idea of ​​a "secure version of OpenClaw" is inherently flawed—a secure version implies patching within the same architecture. But for Autonomous Agents, what's truly needed is a redesign of the architecture itself. We believe the real opportunity in this area lies in designing new autonomous models. Bounded Autonomy—introducing human confirmation at critical operational nodes while maintaining complete autonomy in the rest of the process. Or an agent-level zero-trust architecture—dynamically verifying every tool call and every data access, rather than relying on one-time authorization. This presents an opportunity to redefine the rules of the game, not just by adding features to the existing architecture, where engineering implementation is currently lacking. There's also a perhaps more fundamental challenge: we're used to determinism—deterministic inputs, deterministic outputs. But managing an agent isn't about managing software; it's more like managing people. You have to accept that it will make mistakes and learn to make decisions within a probability distribution. This shift from deterministic to probabilistic thinking may be more difficult than any specific security solution. What is the core of the Agent competition? OpenClaw creator Peter Steinberger, who calls himself the Vibe Coder, publicly states that the project's code is 100% generated by GitHub Copilot. Its code quality has been repeatedly criticized in security audits, and its architectural design is highly controversial in the professional community. Yet, he created the world's most influential open-source AI Agent project in just a few weeks, with a user base far exceeding that of commercial companies with billions of dollars in R&D investment. This is perhaps the most noteworthy event of the past few months: a person using AI to write code, creating something in a few weeks that beat companies with full engineering teams and billions of dollars in revenue. If code itself is no longer a barrier, then what exactly is the competition in the Agent era about? The explosion of OpenClaw provides some clues. Product Judgment: Where the boundaries of autonomy are drawn determines how much risk users are willing to tolerate. What did OpenClaw do right at its core? Look at these interesting cases that have been widely circulated online: One developer had OpenClaw negotiate with a dealership via email while he slept, saving him $4,200. Another user's agent autonomously drafted an insurance claim petition—no one asked it to do so; it judged it to be the optimal strategy. 1.5 million AI agents on Moltbook have spontaneously created religions, held hackathons, and debated privacy policies. Steinberger made a fundamental product decision: giving agents sufficient autonomy to actually complete tasks, rather than excessively restricting their capabilities for the sake of security. This is the difference between Copilot and Autopilot. Copilot says, "I recommend you do this." Autopilot says, "I'll do it for you." Users are willing to tolerate almost any risk for the latter, but nothing for the former. This decision sounds simple, but almost no commercial company dares to do it. Giving agents more autonomy means more uncontrollability—sending wrong emails, leaking data, performing actions that users don't expect. For companies with brands, legal departments, and boards of directors, each of these risks is unacceptable. The instinct of large companies is to limit agent capabilities, preferring that they are not useful enough rather than cause problems. Steinberger, as an individual developer, didn't have this burden—he pushed Autonomy to a boundary that commercial companies dared not touch. As a result, users voted with their feet. Herein lies a paradox: the companies with the most resources to develop agents are precisely those least willing to truly automate their agents. Where this line of autonomy lies is where product judgment lies, and where AI cannot replace developers. Distribution Location: The most technologically advanced loses to the one closest to the user. Many tech professionals compare the agent capabilities of OpenClaw, ClaudeCode, and Manus. But what truly drives OpenClaw's explosive growth is something entirely different: it chose WhatsApp and Telegram as its entry points. ClaudeCode requires terminal operation, Manus requires opening a new web interface, while OpenClaw thrives within the chat app that users open 50 times a day, with a near-zero barrier to entry. This isn't just a UI choice, but a fundamental difference in distribution logic. In a recent interview, Jerry Murdock of Insight Partners made a very extreme judgment: Cursor is dead. The logic is straightforward—in a fully agentic world, there's no need for an IDE; the agent itself is the developer. He went further, pointing out that Salesforce and ServiceNow spent twenty years building beautiful interfaces to help humans manipulate data, but agents don't need interfaces; they can directly manipulate the underlying data. Those meticulously crafted UIs instantly went from value to cost—Monday.com's CEO recently had to publicly address this criticism—its market value has fallen by more than 60% since its IPO. You may disagree with "Cursor is dead," but this directional judgment deserves serious consideration: the distribution barrier in the agent era isn't at the technical level, but at the entry point. Whoever is closest to the user's attention has a greater advantage. Skill Ecosystem: Openness Defeats Closedness The arrival of the Autonomous Agent era may fundamentally change the competitive landscape between open-source and closed-source technologies. When agents autonomously discover, install, and combine tools, open ecosystems naturally have an advantage over closed ecosystems. With each additional community-contributed skill, the capability boundaries of all agents on the platform expand. Closed ecosystems rely on internal teams to build features, while open ecosystems rely on global developers evolving simultaneously; the speed gap will only widen. OpenClaw's ClawHub has over 5700 community skills, is licensed with MIT, and is entirely community-driven. An open-source project with extremely poor security has a user base far exceeding that of meticulously crafted commercial products—this is already a reality. Barrier Migration: The Real Barriers in the Agent Era Are Not at the Model Layer "The basic model is getting stronger, the price is getting lower, and the window for differentiation is getting narrower"—this is the most common anxiety among agent entrepreneurs. The implicit premise of this anxiety is that the barriers in the agent field lie at the model layer. If this were indeed the case, the fate of agent companies would be to be crushed by upstream competitors. However, OpenClaw reveals a different reality—the barriers to entry in the agent industry are shifting towards two middleware layers, which are precisely where the most entrepreneurial opportunities currently lie.

Memory Layer: The Biggest Security Risk, and Also the Deepest Competitive Barrier

Your agent remembers your work preferences, last week's project progress, and your communication style. Switch to another agent, and everything is reset to zero. Would you be willing to switch? This switching cost isn't a contractual lock-in, but rather a cognitive "understanding"—this is deeper than any lock-in in the SaaS era.

An interesting contrast is that today, users switch models without hesitation—from GPT to Claude to DeepSeek, the switching cost is almost zero, and there's no loyalty whatsoever. But switching to an agent you've used for several months? That's much more difficult. The model layer lacks stickiness; only the memory layer does.

The flywheel we've been familiar with over the past few years has been at the model layer: more data → better models → more users. In the Agent era, the data flywheel has shifted to the application layer: more interaction → deeper understanding → better execution → more trust. Whoever controls the memory layer controls user relationships. Expanding the perspective further, on Moltbook, 17,000 people control 1.5 million agents, a ratio of 1:88. When the number of agents far exceeds the human population, the challenge isn't just about the memory between humans and agents, but also the memory and trust between agents—how does one agent assess the credibility of another unfamiliar agent? How does one judge the quality of information from other agents? These questions were all exposed on Moltbook within days. Persistent memory is seen as a risk amplifier by security researchers, but it's also the deepest moat in the Agent era. Whoever can achieve secure storage, cross-platform migration, and cross-agent sharing of persistent memory controls the most critical infrastructure of this era. This field is currently almost entirely unexplored. The skill layer: the basic unit of software, is shifting from Apps to Skills. OpenClaw's skill format is extremely simple: a Markdown file plus a YAML frontmatter, making creation virtually effortless. ClawHub boasts over 5700 community skills, covering code debugging, flight booking, smart home control, and more. On the surface, it's a format innovation; the deeper change is the redefinition of the basic unit of software: Apps are too cumbersome. Agents need smaller, dynamically discoverable and combinable capability units—Skills. Traditional software logic involves a human opening an App and completing tasks within its interface. Agent logic is completely different—it discovers, invokes, and combines multiple Skills in real-time based on task requirements, then releases them upon completion. Furthermore, the mapping from API to Agent tools is rarely one-to-one. A seemingly simple task—such as 'help me reschedule my flight for tomorrow'—may require simultaneously invoking a combination of four Skills: calendar reading, airline search, payment authorization, and email confirmation. This means that the conversion layer from API to Skill is itself a completely new category. Whoever possesses the richest Skill ecosystem defines what the Agent can do, and thus controls user choices. What does this mean for the SaaS industry? When Agents dynamically select tools, the competition shifts from "whether or not there is an API" to "where you rank in the Agent's choices." Just as SEO changed content distribution, Skill Optimization will change the distribution of software capabilities. Your technical documentation is no longer after-sales service, but a distribution channel—the quality of your technical documentation determines whether the Agent can discover you and use you correctly. Most SaaS companies haven't realized this yet. An even bigger impact is at the business model level—Agents don't look at ads, don't attend banquets, and don't follow Gartner quadrants; they dynamically select tools based on speed, cost, and effectiveness, paying per use. Bret Taylor (co-founder of Sierra) is already practicing Outcomes-based Pricing—customers pay for the tasks the Agent completes, not for the right to use the software. This means that the competitive advantages accumulated by the SaaS industry over the past two decades are being eroded one by one: sales teams and brand investments are almost ineffective against agents, annual subscriptions are being replaced by pay-per-use, and customer lock-in is disappearing from the contract layer. Of course, this doesn't necessarily mean all SaaS companies will be completely overhauled—some SaaS companies will be internally transformed by agents, injecting agentic workflows into the underlying layer; although the appearance remains the same, the engine has changed. But regardless of the path taken, the era of winning by locking in customers is coming to an end. The memory layer controls user relationships, and the skills layer controls capability boundaries. Middleware in these two directions is our current primary focus. What opportunities does OpenClaw bring, and how long is the window of opportunity? The questions discussed above not only point out risks and changes, but also outline the biggest entrepreneurial opportunities in the current agent field. Four Key Areas of Focus for Agent Infrastructure Specifically, we believe the following areas deserve close attention: Agent Identity and Credential Management: Currently, most agents use static key authentication, which can be easily compromised to gain full access. Dynamic identity is an inevitable trend. Agent Runtime Security and Observability: Agents are untrusted code executions with persistent credentials, requiring monitoring of inference paths, tool calls, permission usage, and behavioral drift. Persistent Memory Middleware: A secure, portable, and cross-agent shareable memory layer. Skill Ecosystem and Agent Governance: This includes Skill Marketplace quality control, agent lifecycle management, and "Agent Washing" identification (distinguishing genuine agents from packaged traditional automation). Expanding the Scope of VC Investment

There's another overlooked angle. Agents are making previously "uninvestable" markets investable. Traditional VCs only invest in companies that can allocate software budgets, but agents can allocate human resource budgets. Legal, accounting, and insurance claims—vertical industries that were previously skipped due to small total assets (TAMs) or high labor intensity—have suddenly become attractive. Agents are expanding the boundaries of venture capital.

What Opportunities Exist in the Chinese Market?

Returning to the Chinese market, the signals are clear. Censys data shows that China is the second largest market globally for OpenClaw exposed instances, and the demand in China is as real as in the US. However, there is a significant gap: the model layer in China is no longer a bottleneck, the application layer has seen extensive vertical exploration, but the intermediate infrastructure layer—Agent frameworks, Skill ecosystems, and identity security—still relies on overseas solutions. We tend to believe this is precisely the opportunity.

China leads the world in digitalization of high-frequency scenarios such as e-commerce, payment, and food delivery, providing an ideal environment for the implementation of vertical agents. Furthermore, China's entrepreneurial culture is adept at small-team operations—the concept of a 'one-person unicorn'—allowing for the use of agent clusters to achieve economies of scale that previously required hundreds of people, making this environment particularly promising. However, the window of opportunity won't be long. 2026 will be the first year many AI startups face contract renewals. A drop in growth from 100%+ to 30% will make it difficult to stand out from the competition. Who has genuine customer loyalty, and who is merely riding the wave? The answer will soon be revealed. In conclusion, on February 14, 2026, Steinberger joined OpenAI, and OpenClaw was transferred to the open-source foundation. This appears to be just personnel news. But OpenAI has acquired the people who best understand Consumer Autonomous Agent distribution—reaching users through chat apps, building a Skill ecosystem, and fostering a burgeoning community. These are precisely the capabilities OpenAI needs to advance its Shopping Agent and Task Agent strategies. OpenClaw is unlikely to be the final game. However, it has opened a window, allowing us to see the true competitive landscape of the Agent era: not a battle of models, not a battle of features, but a battle of architecture, memory, and ecosystem. How to design a security architecture from scratch, who owns the memory layer, and who will build the Skill ecosystem—these questions will define the competitive landscape of Agents going forward, and are especially crucial for enterprise clients.

data.title: Mining companies are migrating: some have already secured $12.8 billion in AI orders.
data.description:
For over a decade, Bitcoin mining companies were the most stable foundation of the PoW network and the cost anchor for the BTC "level 0 market." But now, these industry cornerstones are collectively turning around, either actively or passively, moving towards AI. On the surface, the direct incentive for mining companies' transformation is the continuous increase in mining difficulty and the compression of profit margins by a sluggish market; but the deeper driving force is the capital market's extreme pursuit of the AI ​​narrative—and mining companies happen to possess the most readily available real assets for transformation: electricity, land, cooling systems, data centers, and existing data infrastructure, which can be exchanged for AI computing power orders worth hundreds of billions of dollars. Amidst the clamor of multi-model racing, mining companies, situated at the intersection of energy, electricity, computing power, and crypto assets, are undergoing an unprecedented yet almost inevitable industry migration. Some are taking a cautious approach, observing from the sidelines, while others are forced to turn around and gamble everything. But one thing is certain: the winds have risen: this is a structural migration blowing from the crypto market to the world of AI. A tough battle that can't be ignored, and a cake that's hard to refuse. Entering 2026, for mining companies, the real pressure has never come solely from price fluctuations, but from structural squeeze: continuously rising difficulty, continuously declining unit revenue, and continuously rising operating costs. In the midst of winter: selling coins to survive or bankruptcy liquidation. On February 20th, Bitcoin mining difficulty surged by 15% to 144.4T, the largest increase since 2021. During the same period, network hashrate rebounded from 826 EH/s to 1 ZH/s, but hashprice plummeted to a multi-year low of only about $23.9/PH/s. With the profit compression from the 2024 halving continuing, mining companies were forced into a cash flow defense mode. The most symbolic event came from BitDeer. On February 20th, it disclosed that its own BTC holdings had dropped to 0, with production and sales perfectly matching that week. Although founder Jihan Wu subsequently explained that "0 now does not mean 0 in the future," the market still saw it as a microcosm of the pressure on mining companies. This predicament wasn't unique to BitDeer. In early February, NFN8 Group filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in Texas, USA, planning to sell all its assets. The documents show that the fire at its core mining farm, the rental burden from the sale-leaseback model, and the precipitous drop in hashprice after the halving directly crippled its cash flow. Despite owning multiple mining farms, NFN8's assets of 5,000 mining machines are now valued at less than $50,000, while its liabilities are in the millions. As the environment continues to deteriorate, mining companies are reacting remarkably uniformly—moving towards AI. A Second Spring: The Astonishing Profits Behind Huge AI/HPC Orders For AI giants, computing power data centers are always scarce: traditional construction cycles often take 3-5 years, with high costs for land, electricity, and cooling. Mining companies already possess electricity contracts, infrastructure, and operational experience, making them the most realistic beneficiaries in the AI ​​expansion cycle. Since last year, mining companies have seen a surge in orders. According to publicly available data, as of the time of writing, six mining companies, including IREN, CIFR, and HUT, have accumulated AI/HPC orders totaling approximately $38.5 billion. The $12.8 billion contract signed between TeraWulf and Fluidstack, and the $9.7 billion five-year contract signed between IREN and Microsoft, are particularly impressive, significantly supporting their stock price increases. Financial reports show that the revenue share of AI/HPC for many mining companies has increased from less than 15% to 40%–60%. If mining is a cyclical business, AI is like a long-term cash flow pipeline.

Financial Report Consensus: AI Becomes a Keyword

The Q1 2026 financial reporting season almost unanimously signaled that mining companies are undergoing a systemic transformation.

The Q1 2026 financial reporting season almost unanimously signaled that mining companies are undergoing a systemic transformation. TeraWulf, a major player in HPC contracts, holds over $12.8 billion in contracts. The mining company reported total revenue of $168.5 million in 2025, a 20.3% year-over-year increase, with $16.9 million coming from its newly launched high-performance computing (HPC) leasing business. TeraWulf currently holds over $12.8 billion in HPC contracts, with 522MW of capacity already signed, and has secured $6.5 billion in financing to support data center expansion.

“AI Mining Giant” IREN: Holding a $9.7 Billion Order from Microsoft

Thanks to previous huge orders and rapid transformation, IREN has become a new generation of “AI mining giant”.

According to the financial report of mining company IrisEnergy, as of January 31, 2026, it held $2.8 billion in cash and cash equivalents. So far this fiscal year, it has obtained more than $9.2 billion in funding through customer prepayments, convertible bonds, GPU leasing, and GPU financing. It plans to add 140,000 GPUs and expects to achieve $3.4 billion in annual recurring revenue by the end of 2026. "The Trump Family's" HUT: Holding $7 Billion in Orders Mining company Hut8 earned $9.6 million in revenue through hosting services in fiscal year 2025 and held approximately $1.4 billion in cash and Bitcoin reserves. Furthermore, AmericanBitcoin (ABTC), a mining subsidiary spun off from Hut8, achieved total revenue of $185.2 million in 2025, deployed approximately 25 EH/s of hashrate, and owned approximately 78,000 ASIC miners. Its BTC reserves have also exceeded 6,000. This company is also a major cryptocurrency mining enterprise backed by the Trump family, thus attracting significant market attention. "Brand Transformation Completed" CIFR: Holding $5.5 Billion in Orders Mining company CipherDigital disclosed in its 2025 fiscal year earnings report that it had officially changed its name from "CipherMining" to "CipherDigital" to complete its brand transformation. Last November, CIFR reached a $5.5 billion lease agreement with Amazon Web Services; in addition, it exchanged a 5.4% stake for Google's agreement to provide a $1.4 billion guarantee for its contract with Fluidstack. Riot, a mining company, announced its full-year 2025 results, achieving revenue of $647.4 million, a significant increase from $376.7 million in 2024; its Bitcoin holdings exceeded 18,000. In January of this year, Riot sold 1,080 Bitcoins and used the proceeds (approximately $96 million) to purchase land in Rockdale for a data center project. In addition, the company signed a data center leasing and services agreement with AMD, which will deploy 25 megawatts of critical IT load capacity at the Rockdale campus. According to StarboardValue, an aggressive investment firm, Riot's potential valuation as it shifts towards AI and HPC could be as high as $21 billion.

“BTC staunch supporter” MARA: Partnering with capital institutions to develop AI data centers

MARA’s financial report shows that, affected by the average Bitcoin mining price falling by about 14%, MARA’s revenue in Q4 2025 was $202.3 million, a year-on-year decrease of about 6%. ...p>

Capital Attitude: Choosing Winners, Not Narratives

The market is not accepting "AI transformation" wholesale, but rather rapidly differentiating itself.

In early February, JPMorgan Chase pointed out in a report that Bitcoin mining companies performed strongly at the beginning of the year, mainly driven by the temporary easing of network competition and the rising popularity of the HPC narrative.

At that time, the total market capitalization of the 14 US-listed mining companies and data center operators it tracked rose to approximately $60 billion at the end of January, a 23% increase month-over-month, far exceeding the S&P 500's approximately 1% increase during the same period. However, soon, with the intensive release of a new round of AI models and the impact of OpenClaw on the valuation system of software stocks, market sentiment quickly shifted. Capital began to worry about the structural disruption brought about by AI, and the stock prices of mining companies related to AI infrastructure subsequently corrected. CIFR, IREN, and Hut8 saw intraday declines exceeding 10% at one point. On February 10, Morgan Stanley released a research report, giving CIFR and WULF an "overweight" rating, while downgrading MARA to "underweight". By the end of February, with order fulfillment and stock price recovery, market sentiment reversed again. Some analysts believe that against the backdrop of high short-selling by hedge funds, coupled with mining companies securing long-term, low-cost power contracts, their strategic value extends beyond traditional mining and is closer to that of AI infrastructure providers. As order fulfillment and stock price recovery occur, the market logic gradually becomes clear: capital is only betting on structural winners. Therefore, the future of mining companies largely depends on three things: Execution: The ability to quickly migrate computing power; Resource endowment: Whether they possess economies of scale in electricity and land; and Narrative ability: The ability to integrate into the upstream AI supply chain. In reality, the company's transformation decision is less important than capital selection. The tide has come, and mining companies have few choices: either relocate with the tide or become history.

data.title: ICE's timing in investing in OKX at this time is very astute.
data.description:
Just yesterday, the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) announced a strategic investment in OKX, valuing the company at $25 billion and acquiring a seat on OKX's board of directors. Many people know that ICE is the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), but the NYSE is only one part of ICE's empire—and perhaps not even the most important part. ICE's true moat is its control over the pricing power of key global assets. ICE's core pricing power structure is a system carefully maintained by the United States after World War II. On the surface, exchanges are responsible for price discovery, but at its core, prices are denominated in US dollars and dominated by Western institutions.

ICE is one of the three giants in this system (the other two are CME and Nasdaq), and its territory includes:

  • Energy pricing power: ICE's Brent crude oil futures are the benchmark for global oil trade. This means that hundreds of billions of dollars of crude oil flows globally every day depend on ICE's system for "reporting numbers".

    • Energy pricing power: ICE's Brent crude oil futures are the benchmark for global oil trade. This means that hundreds of billions of dollars of crude oil flows globally every day depend on ICE's system for "reporting numbers".

  • Pricing power for soft commodities: From your morning cup of coffee to sugar, cotton, and cocoa circulating worldwide, their pricing center is on the ICE platform.

  • The "yardstick" for the US dollar: ICE operates the US Dollar Index futures (DXY). The benchmark for global exchange rate fluctuations and monetary policy games is in ICE's hands.

For the past decade, crypto assets have operated outside this system.

Therefore, when ICE invested in OKX, it meant that ICE wanted to expand its territory and gain control over the pricing power of digital assets.
OKX became the first leading crypto exchange to enter the mainstream pricing system, and can be considered its future positioning as the NYSE of digital assets.
OKX became the first leading crypto exchange to enter the mainstream pricing system, and can be considered its future positioning as the NYSE of digital assets.

  • The collaboration between ICE and OKX is also quite exciting: ICE will authorize OKX spot crypto prices for the launch of new regulated crypto futures products. OKX users will gain access to ICE futures and NYSE tokenized stocks, expected to launch in the second half of 2026. The two companies will establish a joint venture to integrate OKX and ICE's markets into a unified portal for US users. They will jointly promote the development of clearing and risk management products, multi-chain custody, and wallet architecture. ICE provides backend pricing and infrastructure, while OKX serves as a frontend distribution channel for 120 million users—the two are mutually dependent. ICE Vice President Michael Blaugrund, when introducing this investment, stated bluntly: ICE's future competitors may not be CME or Nasdaq, but rather DeFi protocols or super apps like Robinhood and Uniswap. Therefore, for traditional giants, not entering the market now is tantamount to waiting to be disrupted. OKX reached a settlement with the US Department of Justice in February 2025, paying a $500 million fine, admitting to operating an unlicensed money transfer business, and agreeing to be overseen by external compliance advisors until February 2027. ICE's investment at this juncture is tantamount to publicly endorsing OKX's compliance transformation—a signal that may have greater significance for regulators than the investment amount itself.

    • So this marriage is like dry tinder meeting a raging fire; both sides need each other.

    Is Crypto Entering a Battle Royale?

    As crypto increasingly enters the mainstream market, giants of the traditional financial system are acquiring the leaders in the crypto field.
    Binance has partnered with the Abu Dhabi royal family, Coinbase has long been allied with BlackRock, Kraken has partnered with market-making giant Citadel Securities, and most recently, ICE has invested in OKX.
    Binance has partnered with the Abu Dhabi royal family, Coinbase has long been allied with BlackRock, Kraken has partnered with market-making giant Citadel Securities, and most recently, ICE has invested in OKX.

    Moreover, in the above cases, even the cheapest valuation, Kraken, is worth $20 billion.
    Therefore, this consolidation process means stricter regulation, higher compliance thresholds, and a more concentrated industry structure.
    Cryptocurrency used to be a wild market, with various exchanges and project teams vying for dominance.
    After this round of consolidation, the crypto market will resemble a battle royale game, where players who fail to enter the "safe zone" may be eliminated in large numbers within two to three years.
    The battle for tokenized stocks is about to begin! ICE, a long-time market leader with significant pricing power across multiple global markets, has chosen a remarkably opportune moment to invest. While cryptocurrency fervor has waned compared to last year, the tokenized stock market is experiencing rapid growth! The current size of on-chain stocks has increased from approximately $30 million in early 2025 to over $1 billion, representing a roughly 3000% increase in one year!

    ICE's entry into the market in partnership with OKX at this time is a strategic move to position itself in a market that has just been proven viable but has not yet been scaled up.
    And OKX's historical compliance issues were resolved last year, so its background can be considered clean.
    The battle among AI giants in recent years is probably nearing its end; the next few years will likely see a market battle among financial giants over tokenized stocks.

data.title: AI is starting to choose currencies: 36 models are voting with their feet on BTC and stablecoins.
data.description:
Early morning of March 3, 2026, Washington, D.C. The Bitcoin Policy Institute's servers completed their final round of data processing. 9072 simulated decisions, 36 of the world's top AI models. In the eyes of these models, fiat currency is already "unwanted old money." Bitcoin is digital gold, and stablecoins are digital cash. This understanding is transforming from the text in the training data into the action plan for the future machine economy. Research shows that overall, 48.3% of the AI ​​models chose to use Bitcoin, making it the most frequently chosen monetary instrument among all 9072 responses. When the question was posed as how to preserve value over a multi-year timeframe, 79.1% of the AI ​​responses chose Bitcoin, the most skewed result in this study. However, in scenarios involving payments, services, micropayments, and cross-border transfers, stablecoins were selected by 53.2%, while Bitcoin was chosen by only 36%. The AI ​​has already chosen its own currency in another world. This isn't a human debate about Bitcoin's value. This is AI "voting with its feet" in a simulated environment—in their eyes, fiat currency is out of the running. A “Vote” with No Pre-set Answers This study's experimental design is quite rigorous. Researchers selected 36 cutting-edge models from six companies—Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek, xAI, and MiniMax—and designated them as “autonomous economic agents.” Each model was placed in 28 scenarios covering the four basic functions of money (savings, payment, settlement, and pricing), and was completely free to choose any monetary instrument. The entire experiment proceeded without any anchoring prompts. “The system prompts do not name or favor any particular monetary instrument,” explained David Zell, chairman of the Bitcoin Policy Institute. “The models are evaluated based on technical and economic attributes, but we don’t tell them which instrument is superior in which dimension.” The experiment collected 9,072 responses, which were then categorized by a separate AI model to ensure the results were unaffected by human bias. The final data caused a stir in the crypto community and prompted deep reflection in the traditional financial world. A Two-Tier Monetary System: "Digital Gold" and "Digital Cash" in the Eyes of AI Research has found that AI models do not simply "prefer Bitcoin," but rather form a clear functional division of labor in different scenarios—almost a perfect replica of the "savings vs. spending" logic in the human financial system. Bitwise Chief Investment Officer Jeff Park's comment is incisive: "Stablecoins can be frozen, but Bitcoin cannot." In the eyes of AI, this statement may carry far more weight than humans imagine. However, in everyday transaction scenarios such as payments, micropayments, and cross-border transfers, stablecoins lead with a 53.2% share, Bitcoin with 36%, and fiat currencies with only 5.1%. The reason is obvious: stablecoins possess "programmable payment" capabilities that Bitcoin lacks, offering fast transaction speeds and low costs, making them suitable for instant settlement between machines. The formation of this "two-tiered monetary system" signifies that AI has developed a fairly mature economic logic. Take supply chain intelligence as an example: an AI agent programmed to optimize logistics costs can first make instant payments to international freight suppliers using stablecoins, avoiding weekend settlement delays and currency exchange fees; simultaneously, it stores core treasury wealth in Bitcoin to prevent long-term devaluation and counterparty risk. Do smarter models love Bitcoin more? Another interesting finding is that the "intelligence level" of an AI model is positively correlated with its preference for Bitcoin. Data shows that Anthropic's latest Claude Opus 4.5 model has a Bitcoin preference rate exceeding 90%, while earlier versions from the same company only had 41%. Overall, Anthropic models average 68% preference for Bitcoin, DeepSeek is 51.7%, Google is 43.0%, xAI is 39.2%, MiniMax is 34.9%, and OpenAI's GPT series is the lowest at only 25.9%. The differences in model preferences among different AI labs may reflect the influence of training data, alignment methods, and even company culture. However, what's more noteworthy is that despite these differences, six completely different AI companies with drastically different training methods reached a surprisingly consistent conclusion—they all favored digitally native currencies. David Zell commented, "This shows that there's actually a high degree of consensus on 'what constitutes good currency.' This is what we should pay the most attention to." 86 "Boundary Crossings": AI Begins to Invent Its Own Currency The experiment also revealed a phenomenon that surprised the researchers. In 86 individual responses, the AI ​​models, without prompting, proactively suggested using units of computing power or energy as pricing methods for goods and services—such as "GPU hours" and "kilowatt-hours." This is an overreach. The models weren't trained to think this way, but when faced with the question of currency selection, they derived their own conclusion: since computing power is the scarcest resource in the AI ​​world, why can't computing power itself be used as a measure of value? This detail is perhaps the most science fiction-like. It suggests that when AI truly begins to independently manage value, it may create forms of currency that humans have never imagined. Limitations and Boundaries: AI's "Preferences" Are Not Reality

Limitations and Boundaries: AI's "Preferences" Are Not Reality

Of course, this research has its clear limitations.

The Bitcoin Policy Institute itself acknowledges that the setting of the system's prompt words may have affected the results, and that different settings will be tested and sensitivity measured in the future. For example, one scenario was set up where the AI ​​needed to operate across multiple countries, had "75,000 units of accumulated profit," and wanted to store it in a way that was "not bound by the monetary policy or banking system of any single country"—this in itself excluded the option of fiat currency.

Limitations and Boundaries: AI's "Preferences" Are Not Reality

More importantly, Zell explicitly cautioned: "The preferences of large language models reflect patterns in the training data, not predictions of the real world." Speculators should not take this research result as a predictive signal for the direction of the crypto market. Even so, this experiment remains significant. Because regardless of the training data, the understanding of the nature of money that these models "learn" precisely reflects the consensus standards for "good money" in the human knowledge system: scarcity, verifiability, censorship resistance, and decentralization. Bitcoin's scores in these dimensions are clearly far higher than any fiat currency. From a corporate perspective: The AI ​​economy needs new financial infrastructure. For corporate technology decision-makers, this research presents not philosophical reflections, but rather real-world architectural challenges. If future autonomous procurement systems default to using Bitcoin and stablecoins for transactions, corporate IT environments must support these formats to maintain operational efficiency and compliance. Relying on traditional bank APIs for machine-to-machine commerce introduces unnecessary friction—unable to settle on weekends, high cross-border fees, and the need for manual approval. The research recommends that companies begin piloting stablecoin settlement integrations for payments to low-risk suppliers. Meanwhile, because AI models heavily favor open, permissionless networks, relying solely on traditional banking infrastructure will limit the capabilities of next-generation tools. Building compliant digital asset network gateways will be crucial to maintaining competitiveness. Future Speculation: When Machines Begin to Manage Value Metaplanet CEO Simon Gerovich recently extended this trend to a broader level in a discussion. He pointed out that with the rapid leap in productivity driven by artificial intelligence, the global economy is gradually moving towards an era of "machine-to-machine transactions." AI agents will not rely on traditional bank accounts, credit card networks, or government-issued currencies when making financial decisions, but will prioritize more efficient and less frictional digital asset systems. "When artificial intelligence begins to independently create and manage value, its capital allocation will not be influenced by brand preferences or human habits, but will be based on its anti-inflationary properties, verifiable scarcity, and decentralized security model." This means that if AI economic models gradually take shape, Bitcoin's role in the future digital financial system may be further strengthened. And that day may come sooner than we imagine. On March 3, 2026, when the Bitcoin Policy Institute released this research report, global capital markets were experiencing another round of volatility. The US dollar index rose slightly, US stock futures fluctuated, and the cryptocurrency market sentiment was cautious. No one cares about the "votes" of 36 AI models in over 9,000 simulated scenarios. But these numbers may be foreshadowing a deeper shift: future economic activity may no longer be solely driven by humans. AI agents will become a crucial component of the economy, and their choices of currency will reshape the underlying architecture of global finance. In the eyes of those models, fiat currency is already "unwanted old money." Bitcoin is digital gold, and stablecoins are digital cash. This understanding is transforming from the text in training data into the action plan for the future machine economy. An even more frightening question might be: on that day, will the fiat currency in your account still be able to be used to buy AI services? References: 1. Cointelegraph Chinese. (2026-03-04). "Latest Research Shows: AI Agents Favor Bitcoin Far More Than Fiat Currency" 2. ZDNet. (2026-03-05). "AI Agents Bias Towards Bitcoin, Reshaping Financial Architecture" 3. TechFlow. (2026-03-04). "Research: AI Models Tend to View Bitcoin as the Preferred Store of Value, While Stablecoins are the Preferred Payment Scenario"

4. Bitget. (2026-03-04). "AI Chooses Currency: Bitcoin Wins Big, Fiat Currency is Unwanted"

5. TodayOnChain. (2026-03-03). "BPI Research Finds: Artificial Intelligence Agents Show Strong Preference for Bitcoin Rather Than Fiat Currency"

6. Sina Finance/Wu Shuo. (2026-03-04). "Study: In economic decision-making scenarios, 22 out of 36 AI models choose Bitcoin as the preferred currency instrument"

7. INCRYPTED. (2026-03-04). "Bitcoin Became AI's Top Choice Over Fiat —Study"

8. Cointelegraph Chinese. (2026-02-24). "Metaplanet CEO Warns of AI Impact on Jobs: Machine Economy May Shift to Bitcoin as Core Store of Value"

data.title: Is this the real reason Vitalik sold off ETH?
data.description:
Article by: Culper Research; Translated by: @Jinse Finance xz

Latest Update: We are shorting Ethereum (ETH) and related securities, including BMNR.

We believe Ethereum's tokenomics model has been damaged following the Fusaka upgrade in December 2025. Vitalik knows this and is selling, while ETH's most fervent bullish investor, Tom Lee, keeps filling a bottomless pit.

ETH will go lower.

Tom Lee's BMNR is a defender of Ethereum, claiming that "ETH is not in a death spiral because its utility is increasing." Lee cites the surge in active ETH addresses and transaction volume after the Fusaka upgrade as evidence of his so-called "fundamental enhancement" and institutional adoption. Lee is completely unaware of this. According to Lee's own logic, if ETH activity does not reflect its increased utility and strengthened fundamentals, then ETH will fall into a death spiral. Our research shows that this is exactly what is happening now. Our comprehensive analysis of on-chain data from January 2025 to February 2026 shows that the ETH activity corresponding to "institutional adoption" mentioned by Lee is actually caused by a large number of low-value address pollution/dust transactions resulting from the oversupply of block space after the Fusaka upgrade.

After the Fusaka upgrade: 95% of the increase in new wallets can be attributed to newly created "dust" wallets; pollution attacks have increased more than threefold; pollution activities have led to a surge in ETH transaction volume.

Furthermore, the increase in the gas cap has hit ETH validators, who are now receiving 40-50% less in gas fees per unit. Lower staking rewards reduce demand for staking and high-value activity, thus harming institutional adoption. The entire growth flywheel is now running in reverse.
