data.title: How did Circle, with its stock price surging 75%, become the leader in AI payments?
data.description: On February 25th, Circle released its Q4 2025 financial report. Due to better-than-expected results, its stock price surged from $60 to $105 in just a few trading days, a 75% increase. Here's a logic that might be overlooked: Circle may be more than just a "digital dollar infrastructure company"; it's a provider of financial infrastructure for the AI ​​era. During the earnings call, Circle management mentioned something very interesting: currently, approximately 99% of AI agent payments (transactions between AI agents) are completed through USDC. In other words, AI payments are becoming a significant driver of USDC usage growth. In the words of Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire: "We are entering a new world. In the future, there may be billions, even hundreds of billions, of AI agents interacting with each other and performing various economic activities on the internet... and this is just the beginning." This is interesting. As the world's second-largest stablecoin issuer, how did Circle become the biggest beneficiary of AI payments? Today, we'll discuss this question. Stablecoins, the Natural AI Currency Before discussing Circle, let's briefly discuss a question: What are the differences between AI payments and traditional payments? The most obvious difference is that the people spending the money have changed. The entire modern financial system is essentially designed around human organization: swiping cards, transferring money, approvals, reconciliation, and refunds. However, in the AI ​​era, this logic begins to break down. a16z has recently repeatedly mentioned a prediction: If AI agents become the new economic entities, the future internet is likely to become a network of frequent small-value transactions between machines. Simply put, in the agent economy, there are a lot of small-value transactions. Why is this? Because an AI task is often broken down into many steps, completed collaboratively by different agents. For example, if you want to buy a piece of clothing, you might need a clothing selection agent, a price comparison agent, a logistics agent, and so on. Each time another agent is called, a few cents might be charged. This could result in dozens or even hundreds of small transactions for a single task. The problem is that this transaction model is practically unworkable in traditional payment systems. The most obvious issue is cost. Many international internet payment platforms typically charge two fees per card transaction: a percentage fee of approximately 2.9%, and a fixed fee of approximately $0.30. For example, if you buy a $100 item, the transaction fee is approximately $3.20, and the merchant ultimately receives $96.80. In the world of human consumption, this cost is perfectly acceptable. However, problems arise when transactions are conducted by machines. Many AI Agent transactions may only involve a few cents or even a fraction of a cent. In this case, the transaction costs alone will far exceed the transaction amount. Besides costs, there's also the issue of time. In the traditional US banking system, transfers typically take 2-3 business days to settle, and are generally not operational on weekends and at night. But AI Agent economic activities occur 24/7. Imagine a common scenario: at 3 AM, an AI Agent completes a task and needs to immediately pay another Agent, which then calls a third API service. In this chain-like collaboration, real-time settlement is almost a necessity. However, such transactions are difficult to achieve within the current banking system. The problem becomes even more pronounced when cross-border payments are involved. Friction between different countries, currencies, and banking systems makes these high-frequency, low-value transactions even more inefficient. Even setting aside these apparent issues, the most crucial point is that the underlying logic of the two systems is completely different. Traditional payment systems rely on human identity: credit cards are linked to personal identities, bank accounts require KYC (Know Your Customer), and payment authorization requires manual confirmation. AI agents, however, lack all of these features. The reason is simple: machines and humans operate in completely different ways. The credit card system is essentially a command system: you initiate a payment, and the bank executes it. But the AI ​​economy requires a protocol system: rules are written directly into the code, and transactions occur automatically. For example, payments are automatically triggered upon task completion, and agents can only spend within a certain budget. These logics are all executed by the program itself. In other words, when the main participants in internet transactions change from "humans" to "machines," the entire payment system actually needs to be redesigned. This is precisely why stablecoins have recently been re-discussed. From a technical perspective, blockchain provides AI payments with several capabilities that are difficult for traditional financial systems to possess simultaneously. First is programmability. On-chain stablecoins can be controlled by smart contracts to implement conditional payments, budget limits, automatic settlement, and other logic. For example, an AI agent can automatically trigger payments after completing a task. Second is open access. Any software agent can create a blockchain wallet and start trading without needing to apply for a bank account. This is crucial for AI agents. Thirdly, it's a globally unified unit of value. For example, USDC, whether on the Ethereum or Solana network, essentially represents the same dollar value. AI agents can trade freely between different networks. Fourthly, it has a natural audit record. The blockchain ledger is immutable, and every transaction is fully recorded, which is very important for corporate compliance and risk management. Compared to assets with high price volatility like Bitcoin, stablecoins like USDC are directly pegged to the US dollar, making their prices more stable and more suitable as a payment tool. It is against this backdrop that stablecoin issuers like Circle are beginning to find more possibilities in the AI ​​payment system. Why Circle? In fact, many stablecoin companies are exploring AI payments, but the real question is: where exactly does Circle's opportunity lie? The key is that the transition from stablecoins to AI payments is not a simple business extension, but rather an entire infrastructure evolution path. Over the past few years, many companies have talked about AI payments, but few have truly been able to build a complete system. The reason is simple: this involves three layers of capabilities simultaneously: The first layer is stablecoin issuance and liquidity networks. Without a sufficiently large scale of stablecoins, it's impossible to form a global payment foundation. The second layer is efficient on-chain settlement capabilities. Machine-to-machine transactions require a high-speed, low-cost network to complete settlement. The third layer is connectivity with the traditional financial system. Stablecoins must ultimately be able to integrate with the banking system and regulatory framework; otherwise, they will struggle to enter real-world commercial scenarios. Circle's unique advantage lies in the fact that it has already established a foundation in all three layers. Let's look at the first layer: the stablecoin network. Over the past few years, USDC has achieved considerable liquidity within the global digital financial system. By 2025, USDC's circulating supply is estimated at approximately $75.3 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of about 72%. It accounts for nearly 50% of stablecoin trading volume and approximately 25% of market capitalization. Many consider USDC to be gradually becoming one of the representatives of digital dollar infrastructure. This signifies something important: Circle is not entering the AI ​​payment market from scratch. What it truly does is integrate AI payments into the existing USDC network. The ever-expanding network effect of USDC itself forms Circle's first layer of competitive advantage in AI payments. Secondly, there's the on-chain settlement network. In August 2025, Circle launched Arc, a Layer-1 blockchain specifically designed for institutional financial services and the agent economy. Layer-1 can be understood as the underlying system of the blockchain world. All transfers, smart contracts, and stablecoin transactions are ultimately written into the ledger of this chain, where final accounting and security are ensured. Arc's design goal is clear: **minimize transaction friction.** First and foremost, speed. Test data shows that Arc's transaction confirmation time is approximately 700–800 milliseconds. This means transactions are completed almost instantly, offering an experience close to traditional real-time payment networks. Secondly, there's the fee design. Many blockchains require transaction fees to be paid in their own tokens, such as Ethereum using ETH, and the prices of these assets fluctuate significantly. Arc directly uses USDC as the gas fee unit. In other words, transaction costs in the network are naturally denominated in "dollars," making fees more stable and more in line with current consumption habits. Thirdly, there's cost control. Arc proposes a very intuitive design goal: "1 cent, 1 second, 1 click." That is, transaction fees are approximately 1 cent, confirmation is completed within 1 second, and users only need one operation to complete the payment. However, a single fast, low-cost blockchain is insufficient to support the massive volume of transactions between AIs. The real key lies in **supporting micropayments**. To address this issue, Circle launched Nanopayments in March of this year. This is a nanopayment system specifically designed for AI agents. Its core idea is batch settlement: packaging thousands of small payments into a single on-chain transaction, thereby significantly reducing the cost of each transaction. Circle also demonstrated a very intuitive example. They conducted a demonstration with the robotics company OpenMind: A robotic dog named Bits automatically found a charging station when its battery was low, then used USDC to pay for the electricity and complete the charging process, without any human intervention. This is actually a simple example of machine-to-machine payments. At the payment protocol level, the Arc ecosystem is also pushing a new standard—x402. This protocol allows websites or APIs to directly send an HTTP 402 payment request when returning a request. Users or AI agents can complete the payment simultaneously when calling a service. Previously, payment and service were two separate systems. If a website wanted to charge, the typical process was: first register an account, then link a bank card or payment method, and finally use the service. x402 aims to embed payment directly into the internet request. Simply put, it allows websites to collect payment while providing the service, without requiring users to register an account. The whole process is as simple as browsing a webpage. With the infrastructure for AI payments in place, the next major challenge is connecting these transactions with the traditional financial system. This is Circle's third advantage: compliance capabilities. In the crypto industry, Circle has long been considered a relatively conservative company. Compared to some projects that emphasize decentralization and anonymity, Circle has consistently chosen to work closely with regulatory bodies. In the past, this strategy was even seen by some in the crypto community as limiting innovation. However, as stablecoins begin to enter enterprise-level payment scenarios, this strategy has become an advantage. AI payments involve not only individual developers but also a large number of institutions: banks, payment companies, multinational corporations, and financial infrastructure providers. These institutions must meet stringent compliance requirements when choosing payment networks, such as anti-money laundering, fund auditing, and cross-border regulatory rules. In this respect, Circle occupies a relatively unique position. As a stablecoin issuer, Circle holds compliance licenses or registrations in multiple major financial markets, including the United States, the European Union, Japan, Singapore, Hong Kong, and the United Arab Emirates. This means that USDC can be legally used in more regulated environments. Here, we must mention Circle's payment network for enterprises—the Circle Payments Network (CPN). It connects entities including: banking payment service providers (PSPs), virtual asset service providers (VASPs), cross-border payment and clearing institutions, and corporate clients. Through this network, fiat currency and stablecoins can circulate and settle within the same system. This is especially crucial in AI payment scenarios. In the future, transactions between AI Agents will likely be completed directly on-chain using USDC. However, these funds will still ultimately need to be connected to real-world bank accounts. While the overall scale is still in its early stages, the growth rate shows that institutional interest in stablecoin clearing networks is rapidly increasing. As of February 2026, CPN had attracted 55 financial institutions, with an annualized transaction volume of approximately $5.7 billion, a 68% increase compared to the previous quarter. In summary, Circle's bet on AI payments is based on a very clear judgment: The arrival of the AI ​​economy will gradually render existing financial infrastructure obsolete. Stablecoins and blockchain are likely among the few technological architectures that can adapt to this new economic model. This judgment requires two conditions to be met simultaneously. First, the AI ​​agent economy will indeed arrive on a large scale. Consulting firm Kearney predicts that by 2030, the size of agent-mediated commerce could reach $10-12 trillion. Second, traditional payment systems are indeed incompatible with this economic model. The economic infeasibility of micropayments within the credit card system, the structural problems of machine authentication, and the real-time performance gaps in banking systems are all increasingly revealing this. If both of these conditions are met simultaneously, then the future internet is likely to gradually develop a new transaction model: transactions will be completed automatically between machines, and payment will become an integral part of the software. Circle's real bet isn't on a specific product, but on a prediction about the future of the internet: In the AI ​​era, an era where value flows as freely as information, Circle aims to become the TCP/IP of this era.

data.title: Selling Nvidia and buying power plants, a 27-year-old AI investor earned $5 billion in a year.
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.txt

In February 2026, hedge fund Situational Awareness LP submitted its quarterly holdings report, showing that as of the end of the fourth quarter of 2025, the fund's total market value of US stock holdings was $5.517 billion.

Wall Street manages trillions of dollars in assets; $5.5 billion is just a drop in the ocean. But just 12 months ago, this fund's assets under management were less than $400 million, and its founder and chief investment officer is a young man born in 1999.

His name is Leopold Aschenbrenner. He is 27 years old.

In 12 months, he grew the fund from $383 million to $5.517 billion, a more than 14-fold increase. During the same period, the S&P 500's growth was in the single digits. Even more surprising is his portfolio. Looking at the quarterly holdings report, you won't find any of the AI ​​star companies you always see in financial news headlines. Instead, there are fuel cell companies, Bitcoin miners just recovering from bankruptcy, and chip giants being abandoned by the entire market. He says his fund invests in AI, but this doesn't look like the holdings of an AI fund at all; it's more like a madman's shopping list. But this seemingly madman is actually one of the earliest and most profound people in the world to understand how AI will change the world. Before joining Wall Street, he was a researcher at OpenAI, responsible for thinking about how to ensure that AI wouldn't run away when it became smarter than humans; later, he was fired for saying things he shouldn't have, and wrote a 165-page book predicting a future that most people found absurd. Then, he went all in with his entire fortune. Breaking Down $5.5 Billion: What Did He Buy? To understand how brilliant Leopold Aschenbrenner was in investing, the most direct way is to open his portfolio report and read it line by line. His largest holding is Bloom Energy, valued at $876 million, representing 15.87% of his total portfolio. This company makes fuel cells. More precisely, it develops a type of fuel cell called a "solid oxide fuel cell," which can directly convert natural gas into electricity with extremely high efficiency. Founder KR Sridhar was a former engineer on NASA's Mars exploration program and was named one of "the five leading futurists shaping the future" by Fortune magazine. An AI fund has placed its biggest bet on a power generation company. According to Gartner's forecast, global power consumption for AI-optimized servers will surge from 93 terawatt-hours in 2025 to 432 terawatt-hours in 2030, nearly quintupling in five years. The electricity demand from US data centers will nearly triple to 134.4 gigawatts by 2030. The average age of US power infrastructure is already over 25 years, with many components between 40 and 70 years old, far exceeding their design lifespan. In other words, AI needs more electricity than the entire power grid can provide. And the power grid itself is already old and on the verge of collapse. The scarcest resource in the AI ​​era is not chips, but electricity. Bloom Energy's fuel cells can bypass this bottleneck. They don't need to be connected to the grid; they generate electricity directly next to the data center, 24/7. In 2025, Bloom Energy secured a contract from CoreWeave to provide fuel cells for its AI data center in Illinois. Speaking of CoreWeave, it happens to be Leopold's second-largest holding. He holds $774 million worth of CoreWeave call options, plus $437 million in common stock, totaling over $1.2 billion, representing 22% of his total portfolio. CoreWeave is a GPU cloud service provider that transitioned from cryptocurrency mining. In 2017, Mike Intrator and Brian Venturo, along with a few others, got together to mine Bitcoin. In 2018, the cryptocurrency market crashed, and mining became unsustainable. But they had a ton of GPUs. In 2019, they had a brilliant idea: GPUs can do more than just mining; they can also run AI applications. So the company transformed itself, from a mining farm into an arms dealer of AI computing power. On March 27, 2025, CoreWeave went public on Nasdaq, raising $1.5 billion at $40 per share. A company that crawled out of the mining farm became a core supplier of AI infrastructure. What Leopold valued was CoreWeave's large number of GPUs and its deep partnership with Nvidia. In an era where computing power equals productivity, whoever controls the GPUs reigns supreme. But what's truly puzzling is his third largest holding: Intel. With a market value of $747 million, all of it is call options, accounting for 13.54% of the total portfolio. In 2025, Intel was one of the most disliked companies on Wall Street. Its stock price had halved from its 2024 peak, its market share was being eroded by AMD and Nvidia, and it had undergone CEO changes multiple times. Almost every analyst was saying Intel was finished. But Leopold, at this crucial moment, heavily invested in Intel using call options. This was an extremely aggressive move; a successful bet could lead to explosive growth, or a failed one could wipe out everything. What was he betting on? Just two words: contract manufacturing. In November 2024, the U.S. Department of Commerce announced that Intel would receive up to $7.86 billion in direct funding through the Chip and Science Act. The sole purpose of this money is to make Intel a domestic chip foundry in the US, competing with TSMC. Against the backdrop of technological decoupling between the US and China, the US needs a "homegrown" chipmaker. While Intel lags behind, it's the only option. Leopold isn't betting on Intel's technology, but on the national will of the US. The subsequent holdings are even more interesting. Core Scientific, $419 million; IREN, $329 million; Cipher Mining, $155 million; Riot Platforms, $78 million; Hut 8, $39.5 million. These companies share a common characteristic: they are all Bitcoin mining companies. Why would an AI fund invest in a bunch of Bitcoin miners? The answer is simple: because Bitcoin mining companies have the cheapest electricity and the largest data center space in the United States. Core Scientific has over 1300 megawatts of power capacity. IREN plans to expand its capacity by 1.6 gigawatts in Oklahoma. To survive in the fierce competition for computing power, these miners have long since secured the cheapest electricity resources globally, signing long-term power purchase agreements. And now, what AI data centers lack most is precisely electricity and space. In 2022, Core Scientific filed for bankruptcy due to the cryptocurrency crash. It completed its restructuring in January 2024, cutting approximately $1 billion in debt and relisting on Nasdaq. Then, it signed a 12-year, over $10.2 billion contract with CoreWeave to transform its mining farms into AI data centers. To fully commit to this transformation, Core Scientific even planned to sell all its Bitcoin holdings. IREN (formerly Iris Energy) signed a $9.7 billion AI contract with Microsoft, receiving a $1.9 billion upfront payment. Cipher Mining signed a 15-year lease agreement with Amazon. Riot Platforms signed a 10-year, $311 million contract with AMD. Overnight, Bitcoin miners became landlords in the AI ​​era. Now, let's complete this puzzle. Bloom Energy provides electricity, CoreWeave provides GPU computing power, Bitcoin mining companies provide facilities and cheap power, and Intel provides chip manufacturing capabilities in the United States. Add to that his fourth largest holding, Lumentum ($479 million, which makes optical components, core components for interconnecting AI data centers), his ninth largest holding, SanDisk ($250 million, for data storage), and his eleventh largest holding, EQT Corp ($133 million, a natural gas producer that provides fuel for fuel cells). This is a complete AI infrastructure supply chain. From power generation to transmission, to chip manufacturing, to GPU computing power, to data storage, to fiber optic interconnects—he bought into every single link. And another thing he did at the same time made this logic even clearer. In the fourth quarter of 2025, he completely liquidated his positions in Nvidia, Broadcom, and Vistra. These three companies were precisely the star performers in the 2024 AI boom. He also shorted Infosys, one of India's largest IT outsourcing companies. He sold the hottest AI chip stocks and bought unwanted power plants and mining farms. He shorted traditional IT outsourcing because AI programming tools are making programmers more efficient, thus compressing the demand for outsourcing. Every transaction points to the same judgment: the bottleneck of AI is not in software, but in hardware; not in algorithms, but in electricity; not in cloud models, but in the physical world. So the question arises: how did a 27-year-old form this understanding? From the son of an East German doctor to a rebel against OpenAI. Leopold Aschenbrenner was born in Germany to both his parents, who were doctors. His mother grew up in East Germany, and his father came from West Germany; they met after the fall of the Berlin Wall. This family itself bears the mark of a historical rupture—the Cold War, division, and reunion. His later obsession with geopolitical competition may have originated from this background. But Germany couldn't keep him. He later said in an interview, "I really wanted to leave Germany. If you're the most curious kid in the class, wanting to learn more, the teachers won't encourage you; they'll be jealous and try to suppress you." He called this phenomenon "tall poppy syndrome"—whoever grows the tallest gets cut off. At 15, he convinced his parents and flew to the United States alone to attend Columbia University. Attending university at 15 is unusual anywhere. But Leopold's performance at Columbia turned this "outlier" into a "legend." He double-majored in economics and mathematics-statistics, winning every award imaginable, including the Albert Asher Green Memorial Prize, the Romine Economics Prize, and Junior Phi Beta Kappa Honorary Membership. At 17, he wrote a paper on economic growth and existential risk. The renowned economist Tyler Cowen, after reading it, remarked, "When I read it, I couldn't believe it was written by a 17-year-old. If this were an MIT doctoral dissertation, I would be equally impressed." At 19, he graduated from Columbia University as a Valedictorian, the highest honor for undergraduates at the university. In 2021, while the world was still under the shadow of the pandemic, a 19-year-old German stood at Columbia's graduation ceremony, delivering a speech on behalf of all graduates. Tyler Cowen gave him a piece of advice: don't pursue a PhD in economics. Cowen felt the economics academic world had become somewhat "decadent" and encouraged him to do bigger things. Cowen also introduced him to the "Twitter geeks" culture in Silicon Valley, a group of people fascinated by AI, effective altruism, and the long-term fate of humanity. After graduating, Leopold first went to the Forethought Foundation to research long-term economic growth and existential risks. He then joined the FTX Futures Fund, founded by SBF, working with key figures in the effective altruism movement, Nick Beckstead and William MacAskill. His title is "Economist at the Oxford Global Priorities Institute." This experience is significant. It means that before entering the AI ​​industry, Aschenbrenner had spent several years systematically thinking about one question: what kind of events could fundamentally change the course of human civilization? Then, he joined OpenAI. The exact time is unknown, but he joined a special team—the "Superalignment" team. This team was founded on July 5, 2023, and is co-led by OpenAI co-founder Ilya Sutskever and alignment team leader Jan Leike. The goal was to solve the alignment problem of superintelligence within four years—that is, to ensure that an AI far smarter than humans would still obey human commands. OpenAI had pledged to dedicate 20% of its computing power to this team. But a chasm separated promise from reality. Leopold saw something unsettling within OpenAI. He submitted a security memo to the board, warning that the company's security measures were "seriously inadequate" and unable to prevent foreign governments from stealing critical algorithmic secrets. The company's reaction surprised him. Human resources spoke with him, calling his concerns about espionage "racist" and "unconstructive." The company's lawyers interrogated him about his views on AGI and his team's loyalty. In April 2024, OpenAI fired him for "leaking confidential information." The alleged "leak" involved him sharing a brainstorming document about AGI security measures with three external researchers. Leopold stated that the document contained no sensitive information, and sharing such documents internally for feedback was standard practice. A month later, Ilya Sutskever left OpenAI. Three days later, Jan Leike also left. The Super Alignment team was disbanded, and OpenAI's promise of 20% computing power was never fulfilled. A team researching "how to control superintelligence" was disbanded by the very company that was creating superintelligence. The irony of this event cannot be overstated. But for Leopold, being fired was a liberation. He was no longer employed by anyone, no longer needed to carefully choose his words in internal memos. He could speak his mind to the world. On June 4, 2024, he published a 165-page article on a website called situational-awareness.ai. The title was simply "Situational Awareness: The Decade Ahead." To understand Leopold's investment logic, you must first read this lengthy document. Because that $5.5 billion holding is the financial translation of these 165 pages of text. The core argument of this lengthy document can be summarized in one sentence: AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) has a very high probability of being achieved by 2027. This judgment sounded like nonsense in June 2024. But Leopold's method of argumentation is very direct: orders of magnitude. From GPT-2 to GPT-4, AI's capabilities achieved a qualitative leap, transforming from a preschooler into a bright high school student. Behind this leap is an effective computational growth of approximately 100,000 times (5 orders of magnitude). This growth comes from the stacking of physical computing power, the improvement of algorithmic efficiency, and the release of capabilities brought about by the "deconstraint" of models. His prediction is that a similar scale of growth will occur again by 2027. In terms of physical computing power, the computational resources used to train cutting-edge models will be 100 times more than GPT-4. In terms of algorithmic efficiency, it will improve by approximately 0.5 orders of magnitude per year, accumulating to about 100 times over four years. Add to that the "deconstraint" gains, transforming AI from a chatbot into an intelligent agent capable of using tools and acting autonomously—another order of magnitude leap. Three 100-fold increases combined equal another 100,000-fold increase, another qualitative leap. From a bright high school student to surpassing human capabilities. What truly makes this article unsettling is the series of consequences it derives from this prediction. The first consequence: a trillion-dollar computing power cluster. He wrote that in the past year, the talk of Silicon Valley has shifted from $10 billion computing clusters to $100 billion clusters, and more recently, to trillion-dollar clusters. Every six months, a zero is added to the board's plans. By the end of this decade, hundreds of millions of GPUs will be running. This prediction sounded outrageous in June 2024. But in January 2025, the Trump administration announced Project Stargate, a joint investment by SoftBank, OpenAI, Oracle, and MGX, with a plan to invest $500 billion over four years to build AI infrastructure in the United States. The first tranche of funding, immediately deployed, is $100 billion. Construction has already begun in Texas. His "trillion-dollar cluster" plan, written in a lengthy letter, became an official White House plan six months later. The second consequence: a power crisis. How much electricity would hundreds of millions of GPUs require? Leopold's answer was: it would require increasing America's power production capacity by several tens of percentage points. The data confirms his judgment. In 2024, the total capital expenditures of Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Meta exceeded $200 billion, a 62% increase from 2023. Amazon alone spent $85.8 billion, a 78% year-on-year increase. Amazon's capital expenditures are projected to exceed $100 billion in 2025. The vast majority of this money was spent on data centers and power infrastructure. Microsoft even did something unimaginable a decade ago: it signed a 20-year power purchase agreement with Constellation Energy to restart the Three Mile Island nuclear power plant. Yes, that's the Three Mile Island plant, the site of the worst nuclear accident in U.S. history in 1979. The plant will reopen in 2028, renamed the Crane Clean Energy Center, specifically to power Microsoft's data centers. "Powering critical industries, including data centers, requires a constant supply of carbon-free and reliable energy every hour of every day," said Joe Dominguez, CEO of Constellation Energy. "Nuclear power plants are the only energy source that can consistently deliver on this promise." When a software company starts restarting nuclear power plants, you know that electricity has transformed from an infrastructure issue into a strategic resource issue. The third consequence: a geopolitical race. The most controversial part of the lengthy article is Leopold's near-Cold War language, defining the AGI race as a struggle for the survival of the "free world." He harshly criticizes the ineffective security measures at top US AI labs. He vehemently calls for AI algorithms and model weights to be treated as top national secrets. He even predicted that the US government would eventually have to launch a national AGI project similar to the "Manhattan Project." These statements sparked heated debate. Critics argued that he oversimplified the complexities of geopolitics, using a panic narrative to justify unrestrained accelerated development. However, others believed he spoke the truth. Dario Amodei of Anthropic and Sam Altman of OpenAI shared his belief that AGI would soon become a reality. The true value of this lengthy work lies not in the 100% accuracy of its predictions, but in the complete and actionable framework it provides. If AGI really does arrive around 2027, what will the world need before then? Massive computing power. What does computing power need? GPUs. What do GPUs need? Electricity. Where does the electricity come from? From power plants, nuclear power plants, and Bitcoin mining farms with cheap electricity. Where are chips made? At TSMC. But what if the US and China decouple? Then Intel will be needed. How are data centers interconnected? They need optical components—Lumentum. Where is the data stored? It needs storage—SanDisk. You see, this is the logic behind that portfolio report. The 165-page macroeconomic forecast is the map, and the portfolio is the route. Leopold translated this macroeconomic forecast into a portfolio that can be bet real money. Each purchase corresponds to an argument in the 165-page report. Each sale corresponds to an assumption that he believes the market is mispriced. But a map alone is not enough. In the real market, you also need one more thing: to continue believing you are right when everyone else says you are wrong. This ability underwent its most severe test on January 27, 2025. The release of DeepSeek-R1 on January 27, 2025, sent shockwaves through Wall Street. This model's performance approached that of OpenAI's o1, but at a cost 20 to 50 times cheaper. Even more astonishing was that its predecessor, DeepSeek-V3, reportedly cost less than $6 million to train, using the Nvidia H800 chip, which was subject to US sanctions and had limited performance. The market's logic collapsed instantly. If the Chinese can train top-tier models with $6 million and crippled chips, what about the hundreds of billions of dollars that American tech giants spend annually? What's the point of those trillion-dollar computing cluster projects? Will GPU demand plummet? Panic spread like wildfire. Nvidia's stock price plunged nearly 17%, wiping out $593 billion in market capitalization in a single day—the largest single-day market capitalization loss in Wall Street history. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index plummeted 9.2%, marking its biggest single-day drop since the March 2020 pandemic panic. Broadcom fell 17.4%, Marvell fell 19.1%, and Oracle fell 13.8%. The decline began in Asia, spread to Europe, and finally exploded in the United States. The Nasdaq 100 index alone lost nearly a trillion dollars in market capitalization in a single day. Silicon Valley venture capital guru Marc Andreessen tweeted that DeepSeek was the "Sputnik moment" for AI, saying, "This is one of the most amazing and impressive breakthroughs I've ever seen, and as an open-source project, it's a gift to the world." For Leopold's fund, this day should have been a disaster. His holdings were entirely in AI infrastructure stocks, while the market was questioning the entire logic behind AI infrastructure. According to Fortune magazine, an investor at Situational Awareness LP revealed that during the market panic sell-off, a large tech fund called to inquire about the situation. Their response was five words: "Leopold says it's fine." Why was Leopold so calm? Because in his view, the emergence of DeepSeek not only didn't refute his logic, but rather confirmed it. A core argument in his lengthy statement is that the progress of AI will not slow down, but will only accelerate. The improvement of algorithm efficiency is one of the three major engines driving the development of AI. DeepSeek trained a stronger model with less money and weaker chips, which precisely proves that algorithm efficiency is rapidly improving. Higher algorithm efficiency means that the same computing power can produce stronger AI, which will stimulate more computing power demand, rather than reduce it. To use the framework from his lengthy book: DeepSeek didn't prove "we don't need that many GPUs," but rather that "every GPU has become more valuable." When you can train better models with less money, you don't stop; you train more, bigger, and stronger models. The panic stems from the fear that "demand will disappear." But those who truly understand AI know that cost reductions never eliminate demand; they only create greater demand. Leopold bought against the trend during the panic. The market quickly proved him right. Nvidia and the entire AI sector rebounded rapidly in the following weeks, returning to levels higher than before the crash. In the world of investing, conviction is the scarcest asset. It's not that forming a belief is difficult, but rather that persisting in a belief when everyone says you're wrong is almost counterintuitive. The story of Leopold Aschenbrenner can certainly be simplified into a feel-good tale of a genius boy becoming rich overnight. But focusing solely on the money misses the true value of this story. What he truly did right was shift his attention from the code and model parameters on screens to the chimneys of power plants, the substations of mines, and the fiber optic cables spanning continents. In 2024, the world was discussing how powerful GPT-5 would be, how realistic Sora could generate videos, and when AI would replace programmers. These discussions were certainly important. But Leopold asked a more fundamental question: How much electricity do these things need? Where does the electricity come from? This question sounds too simplistic, but it is precisely this simple question that points to the biggest investment opportunity in the AI ​​era. AI is growing at an exponential rate, while the physical infrastructure supporting it remains stuck in the last century. Leopold saw this gap. Then, following this gap, they traced it all the way to the ends of the physical world. Each step started from a physical bottleneck, found a company that solved that bottleneck, and then placed their bet. The essence of this methodology isn't new. During the California Gold Rush in the 19th century, the people who made the most money weren't the gold miners, but those who sold shovels and jeans. Levi Strauss made his fortune during that time. But knowing this principle is one thing; implementing it in the AI ​​era is quite another. To execute this, you need two abilities simultaneously: a deep understanding of technological trends, knowing the development path and resource requirements of AI; and a concrete understanding of the physical world, knowing where electricity comes from, how data centers are built, and how fiber optic cables are laid. The former requires experience in OpenAI's labs, while the latter requires a willingness to delve into the details, studying the electricity contracts of a bankrupt mining company. Technicians understand AI but not the electricity market. Financial professionals understand the market but not the physical constraints of AI. Leopold possesses both. But more important than ability is perspective. One sentence from his lengthy treatise is often quoted: "You can see the future first in San Francisco." The subtext is: the future is not evenly distributed. The essence of investing is finding price mismatches in a future that has already arrived but is not yet evenly distributed. Leopold witnessed the capability curve of AI firsthand in OpenAI's labs. He knew GPT-4 was not the end but the beginning; he knew there would be larger models, more computing power, and even more frenzied capital investment to come. Meanwhile, the market was still debating whether "AI is a bubble." This is the mismatch. What he did was turn this mismatch into $5.5 billion.

data.title: Coinbase execs face lawsuit alleging insider profits tied to compliance failures
data.description:
Coinbase hit with fresh shareholder lawsuit as legal troubles pile up

A Coinbase shareholder has filed a derivative lawsuit against several of the exchange’s top executives and board members, accusing them of failing to properly oversee compliance and disclosure practices. The complaint argues that these alleged oversight failures exposed the company to regulatory penalties while insiders continued to benefit financially.

🚨JUST IN: @COINBASE CEO AND EXECS FACES SHAREHOLDER LAWSUIT OVER DISCLOSURE AND COMPLIANCE FAILURES

A Coinbase shareholder has filed a derivative lawsuit against CEO Brian Armstrong and several members of the company's board and executive team, alleging they misled investors… pic.twitter.com/9K7Pw0GdxP

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 5, 2026

Filed Tuesday in the US District Court for the District of New Jersey, the lawsuit was brought by shareholder Kevin Meehan on behalf of Coinbase Global. It names CEO Brian Armstrong, co-founder Fred Ehrsam, chief legal officer Paul Grewal, chief financial officer Alesia Haas, and several current and former directors and executives.

The complaint claims that between Coinbase’s April 2021 direct listing and June 2023, the company’s leadership made false or misleading statements about regulatory and compliance risks. According to the filing, these alleged failures ultimately contributed to enforcement actions against the exchange.

VanEck CEO: Bitcoin Is Making a Bottom

VanEck CEO Jan van Eck said in a March 3 interview with CNBC that Bitcoin's 6% single-day gain helped drive a broader rally across the crypto sector, including related companies such as Coinbase and Circle. He noted that the current rebound… pic.twitter.com/r5d3TFIfjp

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) March 5, 2026

Among them was a $100 million settlement with the New York Department of Financial Services in early 2023 over deficiencies in Coinbase’s anti–money laundering program. The company also faced a $5 million penalty from New Jersey’s Bureau of Securities related to the alleged listing of unregistered securities.

Meehan is seeking damages on behalf of Coinbase as well as governance reforms and the clawback of compensation and profits allegedly earned by insiders while the compliance problems persisted. Because the case is structured as a derivative lawsuit, any financial recovery would go to the company itself rather than directly to shareholders.

Another legal battle for the crypto exchange

The lawsuit adds to a growing list of legal challenges facing the largest US-based cryptocurrency exchange. The complaint accuses the defendants of breach of fiduciary duty, abuse of control and unjust enrichment tied to what it describes as systemic compliance failures.

Coinbase did not immediately respond to requests for comment regarding the new filing.

The case also follows other shareholder litigation targeting the company. In January, a Delaware judge allowed a lawsuit alleging insider trading by Coinbase directors — including Armstrong and board member Marc Andreessen — to move forward despite an internal investigation clearing the executives.

🚨BREAKING: Coinbase CEO & Board Face $2.9 BILLION Insider Trading Lawsuit

A Delaware judge just DENIED the motion to dismiss a massive lawsuit against Brian Armstrong and other Coinbase execs.

The allegation? They dumped $2.9 BILLION in stock right after the direct listing -… pic.twitter.com/PZDwpT8YHb

— Predictivemoney (@Predictivemoney) February 2, 2026

Another proposed class action filed in May 2025 claimed investors were harmed after Coinbase disclosed a user data breach and an alleged violation of an agreement with the United Kingdom’s Financial Conduct Authority. The disclosures triggered a sharp drop in the company’s share price, prompting fresh scrutiny from investors.

data.title: Stablecoins in AI Agents: The Underlying Architecture of the Next Generation Financial Payment System
data.description:
Source: insights4vc, Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

As of March 2026, the global financial landscape is characterized by a systemic shift from human-centric digital commerce to an autonomous agent commerce model. This shift represents the most significant reshaping of the transaction lifecycle since the advent of internet-native payment gateways.

Agentic commerce is defined as an economic system in which software entities, as independent entities, discover services, negotiate complex terms, and complete financial settlements with minimal human intervention. This shift is not merely an evolution of the user interface, but rather a replacement of synchronous, session-based authorization methods with asynchronous, authorization-based, cryptographic delegation.

Underlying this new economy is a complex protocol architecture that bridges the gap between Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning and rigid financial finality. Stablecoins, especially USDC, have become programmable mediums of exchange and are deeply integrated into traditional bank card networks and native blockchain settlement layers. The market has converged on a unified model, viewing retail goods and metered machine services as different variations of the same underlying transaction architecture, rather than as separate development paths. As of early 2026, agent commerce has moved from the prototype stage to institutional applications. Artificial intelligence agents (AI agents) are no longer merely seen as decision support tools; they are increasingly viewed as economic agents capable of initiating procurement, authorizing payments, and settling value transfers within enterprise workflows. Changes between 2025 and 2026

1) Standardized Discovery of Agents (UCP)

The Common Commerce Protocol (UCP) standardizes how agents discover the /.well-known/ucp services that merchants can provide through manifest files. In effect, it is gradually becoming a common interface layer for "machine-readable commerce," similar to the role HTTP plays in information exchange.

2) Verifiable Intent Becomes a Mandatory Requirement (AP2)

The Proxy Payment Protocol (AP2) introduces verifiable digital credentials (VDCs) to link agent behavior to explicit, deterministic human authorization (who authorized what, and under what conditions), rather than relying on probabilistic model behavior.

3) Machine payments return HTTP 402 status code (x402)

The x402 protocol directly uses the HTTP status code "402 Payment Required" for machine-to-machine (M2M) payments in API calls. It has been reported that by the end of 2025, the adoption rate of this protocol has significantly increased, thus validating the "pay-as-you-go" model without the need for traditional checkout processes.

4) Bank card networks mark agents as primary entities

Visa (Intelligent Commerce) and Mastercard (Agent Pay) extend network token primitives to agent environments. Their goal is to bind payment credentials to specific agents and satisfy consumer-defined constraints while retaining existing risk, fraud, and dispute resolution mechanisms.

... 5) Stablecoin Settlement Becomes an Institutional-Grade Payment Track Visa's expansion of USDC settlement signifies that stablecoins are shifting from their "native cryptocurrency" use to institutional settlement, enabling 24/7 liquidity. Its main operational impact is faster cash flow and reduced collateral friction for participants. 6) Regulatory Oversight Begins to Strengthen Border Controls The regulatory measures taken by the United States in 2025 and subsequent guidance in 2026 have been clearly defining expectations for stablecoin issuers (reserve backing, permitted activities, restrictions on similar yield characteristics), pushing the stablecoin payment system closer to regulated financial market infrastructure.

7) Microtransactions Become Economically Feasible

Stablecoins' native batch processing and fee-free execution design make payments below one cent a reality, enabling software business models that are difficult to achieve with bank cards, such as pay-per-inference, pay-per-call, and pay-per-meter billing.

8) New Trust Layers Formed (Identity, Reputation, Sybil Attack Defense)

Identity registration (e.g., ERC-8004-style methods) aims to regulate proxy identity and reputation, thereby reducing the risk of merchant impersonation and Sybil attacks in autonomous commerce.

Identity registration (e.g., ERC-8004-style methods) is designed to regulate proxy identity and reputation, thereby reducing the risk of merchant impersonation and Sybil attacks in autonomous commerce.

...

9) The Compliance-First Orchestration Model (CPN) Emerges

Circle Payments Network (CPN) positions itself as a coordination layer for institutions, embedding compliance requirements (such as travel rule data) and real-time foreign exchange into the stablecoin payment process.

10) A Unified Transaction Architecture Replaces the "Two Channels"

The industry is moving towards a layered model where retail checkout and API/service transactions share the same fundamental elements: discovery, authorization/instruction, credentials, settlement, identity, and dispute or recourse mechanisms.

Stablecoin Orbit

By 2026, stablecoins, especially USDC, will increasingly resemble programmable settlement infrastructure rather than speculative crypto assets.

In fact, as on-chain registration instruments, their transaction and issuance conditions can be enforced by software, which makes them suitable as settlement layers for both institutional and machine-native transactions.

Average Supply of Stablecoins (By Stablecoin Type)

Why Stablecoins Can Serve as a Settlement Layer

These advantages can be summarized into four specific characteristics:

  • Determinism and Finality. On fast finality networks, transfers can achieve irreversible finality in less than a second, reducing operational uncertainty inherent in probabilistic settlement models. 24/7 Settlement. Stablecoin settlement systems support uninterrupted settlement, including weekends and holidays. For banks and payment intermediaries, this reduces reliance on large pre-funded buffers used in proxy settlements and multi-hop settlements. Atomic Effects in the Payment Process. Orchestration layers such as CPNs, combined with stablecoin issuance across multiple currencies, support quote-based FX transactions that can be executed during the payment process. This allows atomic swaps such as USDC to EURC to be completed as part of a single transaction flow without separate funds management steps. Conditions Plus Default Escrow. Stablecoins can be locked in escrow accounts and released only when specific conditions are met, such as cryptographically proving task completion. This is particularly important for automated transactions, where payment and fulfillment can be closely intertwined. The largest stablecoin adoption in 2026 is often invisible to end users. In this model, stablecoins are used for institutional settlement, while the consumer experience remains the same as native bank card payments. For example, Visa's USDC Settlement Expansion program aims to allow card issuers and acquiring partners to settle certain debts in USDC while maintaining the consumer card-holding experience. The front end still enjoys familiar consumer protection and dispute resolution mechanisms, while back-end liquidity flows faster and is virtually continuous. The Impact of "So What?" For Banks: Stablecoin-native settlement shifts business operations from a limited weekday window to near 24/7 availability, significantly improving cash management, liquidity operations, and automated reconciliation. For Merchants/Platforms: To capture agent-driven needs, merchants require deterministic, machine-readable interfaces (UCP-style checklists). Product discovery, comparison, and routing increasingly occur between agents, rather than between humans and browsers. For AI Agent Builders: The focus has shifted to auditability. Embedding verifiable intent (AP2 requirements + credentials) into the runtime ensures operations are traceable, constrained, and compliant with regulatory and network standards. Direct Stablecoin Payments and Dispute Resolution Gap Direct stablecoin payments, including API-native processes like x402, present a core challenge: blockchain transfers are irreversible by default and lack native chargeback mechanisms. To meet the expectations of card networks at the point of interaction, additional layers must be added to provide "recourse."

    Stablecoin Model

    Three emerging methods are recurring:

    • Reputation-based responsibility. Instead of using legal chargeback mechanisms, service failures are handled through identity and reputation layers. If a service provider fails to provide services after receiving payment, the buyer's agent can submit evidence, and the service provider may be penalized with reputational damage or being banned from accessing future agent traffic. Refund and Reversal Agreements. The second approach is a smart contract-based refund mechanism, where the refund process is standardized and enforceable on-chain. Some ecosystem proposals have also explored reversible transactions in institutional environments, especially where compliance and participant permissions are more stringent. Verification Predicates with Escrow Release. For high-value B2B and B2C transaction flows, designs often shift towards escrow before payment. Funds are only released when explicit verification conditions are met, for example, task execution proves that a pre-agreed set of conditions is satisfied. Defining and Unifying Agent Transaction Architectures. The rise of agent commerce requires a unified conceptual framework to explain the diverse needs of retail checkout and metered payment services. In this model, the "proxy transaction architecture" provides a layered approach, where each layer deals with a specific aspect of economic exchange, regardless of the type of performance. Proxy commerce is a paradigm in which autonomous software agents complete the entire business lifecycle—from discovering callable services to assessing counterparty credit, placing orders, and final settlement—without step-by-step human intervention, but still based on verifiable human instructions. Layered Proxy Transaction Architecture This technical architecture consists of six distinct layers, each providing key services to the autonomous economy.

      Unified Flowchart: Retail Checkout and Metering Services

      The table below shows how seemingly disparate use cases map to the same layer of a unified architecture.

      ... The core difference between these two processes lies not in the architecture, but in the parameterization settings of each layer. For retail operations, this architecture is optimized for high protection and moderate latency. For machine services, however, it is optimized for high throughput and default finality. Trust and Identity In an autonomous economy, institutional adoption depends on a hard requirement: the ability to prove that a specific legitimate person approved a specific payment. Without this proof, no matter how good the agent's user experience becomes, businesses cannot meet audit, risk, and compliance thresholds. From an operational perspective, a "trusted agent" is more than just reliable software. It's an encrypted, authenticated identity recognized by payment networks or official channels, strictly bound to specific merchants and checkout contexts to prevent replay or redirection, and anchored through human authorization events (e.g., via cryptography/FIDO2) to ensure persistence and non-repudiation of authorization. The ecosystem is evolving towards an ERC-8004-style trust stack built on portable identities, reputation signals, and independent verification (such as re-execution, zero-knowledge machine learning, trusted execution environments, or oracles) so that trust can scale with increasing risk value. An unresolved gap lies in legal accountability: mapping encrypted agent identities to legally responsible entities. Even with increased regulation of stablecoin issuers, agent liability in practice is still determined by record-keeping structures and audit trails (such as AP2-style requirements) to determine whether an action is truly "authorized" in a legal sense.

      The Real Advantage of Stablecoins

      Transaction Lifecycle Stages of Bank Card and Stablecoin Payment Systems

      Noah, Partner at a16z Crypto

      Levine offers a useful framework, arguing that the "stablecoin vs. bank card" debate is often flawed: bank cards will likely continue to win most agent-driven consumer transactions because they are tied to credit, fraud instruments, and dispute resolution rights, while the real entry point for stablecoins lies in situations where new merchants and services are too risky, too short-lived, or too early to be guaranteed. Timing is crucial because the supply of these "non-traditional merchants" is exploding: GitHub reports 36 million new developers joining in 2025, and Y Combinator has publicly stated that about a quarter of the codebases in the W25 batch are 95% AI-generated, which can serve as a metric for the speed at which new software supply emerges and iterates. Meanwhile, platforms like Bolt are attracting non-developers to participate in the creation of production software, accelerating the formation of small, informal "micro-merchant" services. These services can sell functionality directly through APIs, without going through a website or sales process. In this environment, the limiting factor is no longer settlement speed, but rather underwriting and recourse: if a service has no operational history, unclear ownership, and unstable operating time, it is only natural for traditional payment processors to refuse service, as the responsibility for refunds and the risk of fraud must be borne by one party. Noah Levine argues that stablecoins fill this gap not because they are “better than bank cards,” but because they are far better than being completely unable to benefit from a multitude of unreliable emerging services. This shifts the forward-looking question from “which track is cheaper?” to “which system can assess reliability, bind identity, and price risk before an agent remits irreversible funds?” The next phase of agency commerce will be won by systems that enable machines to recognize three elements: verifiable merchant identity, verifiable user authorization, and verifiable fulfillment signals. If these proof mechanisms are weak, the market will default to custodial models, strategy wallets, reputation systems, and intermediary models, which can absorb risk and gradually guide merchants towards bank card-like protection. If these proof mechanisms are robust enough, stablecoin settlements could function like native machine primitives, enabling new unit economy models such as pay-per-use and pay-per-inference, without requiring merchants to have accounts from the outset. The core implication is subtle yet crucial: the long-term opportunity lies not in replacing Visa or Mastercard, but in building the missing layers of trust and underwriting for millions of “merchants who don’t yet exist,” enabling agents to securely route value to them at internet speeds. Adoption Signals and Near-Term Roadmap By March 2026, the market will no longer be primarily narrative-driven. We now have production deployments, clear specifications, and institutional pilot projects, all indicating genuine adoption. Key Evidence Used by the Institution: 1) The public specifications have transitioned from the draft stage to a tagged official release stage. UCP 1.0 (January 2026) and AP2 v0.1.0 (September 2025) have been released as versioned versions on GitHub, marking the stabilization of the interface and naming conventions. 2) Real-time volume and usage are important information. According to Reuters, as of November 30, 2025, Visa reported monthly stablecoin settlements at an annualized rate of $3.5 billion, rising to approximately $4.5 billion by mid-January 2026. Launched in May 2025, x402 aims to embed payment functionality directly into HTTP requests using long-dormant 402 status codes. Within just a few months, it processed over 100 million payments across APIs, applications, and AI agents, enabling paid API calls and autonomous, on-demand purchases of computing and data resources. 3) Institutional pilot projects are no longer just talk. Mastercard has completed a real-time AI agent payment campaign in Singapore in partnership with DBS Bank and UOB. Visa has expanded its USDC settlement pilot project to Cross River Bank and Lead Bank on Solana, increasing the number of genuine institutional participants.

    4) Embedded product launches are reaching consumers

    OpenAI's "Instant Checkout" in ChatGPT is an early mass-market implementation of ACP and Stripe Shared Payment Tokens, pushing proxy checkout to the mainstream interface.

    5) Regulatory stance is gradually translating into concrete actions.

    The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) Announcement 2026-3 (February 25, 2026) represents a formal implementation step related to the GENIUS Act, reinforcing the direction of considering stablecoin issuers as part of the core financial infrastructure.

    The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) Announcement 2026-3 (February 25, 2026) represents a formal implementation step related to the GENIUS Act, reinforcing the direction of considering stablecoin issuers as part of the core financial infrastructure.

Near-term Outlook to 2026

Standardization is accelerating at the "discovery" and "syntax" levels

It is expected that by the end of 2026, merchants will drive the adoption of common standards (UCP and adjacent agent connection layers, such as MCP) to reduce the cost of supporting multiple AI platforms and runtimes.

Fragmentation still exists in "trust" and "reputation"

Identity and trust registration are still in a competitive phase.

Competition among the following parties is likely to continue: Extending the identity and responsibility framework of the bank card era to a network-dominated model of agents (Visa, Mastercard) Decentralized solutions (including ERC-8004 registries) are driving the realization of on-chain reputation and composable identity primitives A lasting dual-track reality is emerging The business governance track (tokenized cards, fiat currency track) is likely to dominate most retail goods and consumer goods businesses due to built-in refund mechanisms, fraud protection, and mature liability regimes.

  • Native Settlement Track (Issuing Stablecoins via a model similar to x402): Potentially dominating the machine economy and high-frequency API markets, where instant finality and sub-cent economic benefits are more important than consumer dispute frameworks.

    • Additional: System Reference Architecture

    To achieve a unified model, we focus on specific message flows. While the business logic changes, the underlying fundamentals remain consistent: discovery, authority status, verifiable intent, credential delegation, and verifiable settlement events.

    Process 1: Agent-Assisted Retail Purchases and Consumer Protection

    Objective: Consumer-facing agents complete retail purchases on behalf of users, while retaining refunds, fraud protection, and existing dispute resolution mechanisms through tokenized bank card channels.

    1. Merchant Capability Discovery (UCP). The agent retrieves the merchant's UCP list from /.well-known/ucp. This list publishes supported features (e.g., dev.ucp.shopping.checkout) and available payment processors (e.g., Stripe, Shop Pay).

    2. Authorized Checkout Status (MoR). The agent calls the merchant's commerce API to build the shopping cart. The merchant remains a MoR and returns an authoritative checkout object, including item details, taxes, shipping options, total amount, and expiry date rules.

    3. Authorization Generation and Destination Binding (AP2). If AP2 is supported, the checkout response includes a merchant signature promising to adhere to the terms (prices, items, shipping, refund policies, etc.). The user views the shopping cart in a trusted user interface and confirms it with a strong authenticator (e.g., password/biometrics), generating an encrypted cart authorization. Credential Delegation (Stripe SPT + Network Token) The agent requests a shared payment token (SPT) from the user's wallet. Stripe configures a ranged network token (Visa or Mastercard) based on the user's authorization constraints. The agent receives an abstraction of the available payment credentials, not the raw PAN. Execution and Settlement (Bank Card Track, Modern Vault) The agent submits the following to the merchant's /complete endpoint: the SPT (credential token) and the user-signed cart authorization (proof of intent). Merchants charge through their standard card payment integrations (e.g., Stripe). Settlement takes place within the existing card payment system. Where supported, participating institutions may choose to use stablecoins for settlement (modernizing fund management) without altering consumer protection measures. In the event of a dispute (non-delivery, misrepresentation), the signed shopping cart authorization letter plus the merchant's signed checkout commitment will constitute an auditable and non-repudiable record of evidence for use by the card issuer and network arbitration. Key Feature: The agent never handles the original voucher, and the possibility of dispute remains due to the transaction being terminated within the card network's protection framework. Process 2: Paid API Requests Using HTTP Native Payments Objective: The autonomous agent uses network native primitives to pay for requests for computational or data resources, where the unit of sale is an HTTP call.

      1. Initial request and challenge (HTTP 402). The proxy sends a normal HTTP request to the protected API endpoint. The server responds with a 402 Payment Required.

      2. Payment request discovered.

      3. Payment request discovered.

        4. The response includes a PAYMENT-REQUIRED header (or equivalent metadata) specifying:

    • Price (e.g., 0.10 USDC),

    • Network (e.g., Solana),

    • Payment to (recipient)

    1. Intent checking and payload building (enforcement). The proxy's policy wallet compares the request against pre-approved intent authorizations (limits, spending caps, allowed endpoints) for that provider. If the requirements are met, it constructs and signs a payment authorization payload (e.g., typed data in EIP-712 format) that matches the terms of the server request. Retry after providing proof (payment signature header). The proxy retryes the same HTTP request, including a PAYMENT-SIGNATURE header with the signed authorization payload. Verification and Finality (Assisted Execution). A third-party service provider (e.g., an institutional developer payment platform) verifies the signature off-chain and executes the on-chain transfer, charging a fee to keep the user experience fast and achieve economic efficiency below one cent. Delivery and Confirmation (Settlement Receipt). The server returns the requested data in a 200 OK response, along with a PAYMENT-RESPONSE header containing a settlement receipt (e.g., transaction hash). Key Features: No merchant account registration required; payments are embedded in HTTP interactions and conclude with on-chain settlement events. Final Perspective: Restructuring the transaction lifecycle of software proxies is a structural shift. By 2026, the performance of financial institutions, merchants, or platforms will increasingly depend on their ability to interoperate within proxy transaction architectures (discovery, authorization/delegation, credentials, settlement, identity, and recourse). Stablecoins are increasingly becoming a programmable settlement logic, which traditional fiat ledgers do not inherently provide. The main execution risk over the next 18 months lies in governance lag: the scaling speed of autonomous systems will outpace the development speed of the accountability, identity, and auditing frameworks required for their secure operation.

data.title: Justin Sun reaches $10 million settlement with the SEC
data.description:
Author: Dong Jing, Wall Street News

The two-year enforcement case between the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Justin Sun has seen substantial progress.

On March 6, according to media reports, based on the proposed final ruling filed with the New York Federal Court on Wednesday (March 4), the SEC will drop all remaining charges against Justin Sun and related entities on the condition that Rainberry (a company related to the Tron blockchain) pays a $10 million civil penalty. This settlement marks the imminent conclusion of one of the SEC's most representative enforcement actions against the crypto industry.

Justin Sun posted on March 5 that he has confirmed that the SEC has taken action to dismiss all charges against him, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. He also said he looks forward to working with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to develop guidelines and regulations for the future development of cryptocurrencies. Analysis points out that the settlement in this case will directly impact the market by eliminating the regulatory uncertainty that has long hung over the Tron ecosystem. Rainberry agreed to accept the settlement without admitting or denying the allegations, a standard clause in SEC enforcement actions. However, the proposed ruling still requires final approval from the U.S. District Judge for the Southern District of New York. The SEC "biasedly" dismisses remaining claims. Reports indicate that, according to the proposed agreement, the SEC will dismiss the remaining charges against Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. The proposed agreement states that the remaining claims against Rainberry will be dismissed "biasedly," and the final judgment will also dismiss all claims against Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. "Biased" means the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will be unable to bring similar cases against the same conduct in the future. The SEC has reviewed and approved the settlement terms, which are reflected in the consent form and the proposed final judgment. Rainberry, Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation have agreed to the final judgment taking effect. This settlement comes against the backdrop of a significant shift in U.S. crypto regulatory policy. Former SEC Chairman Gary Gensler's hardline stance in applying securities laws broadly to the digital asset industry has been a hallmark of the regulatory body's readjustment of its overall approach to crypto enforcement since his departure. Justin Sun has recently garnered attention for his connection to World Liberty Financial—a crypto project linked to allies of President Trump. The proposed settlement does not involve these activities, but its resolution would remove the most prominent regulatory cloud hanging over the TRON founder and his company. The SEC filed a lawsuit against Sun in 2023, accusing him of selling unregistered securities and manipulating the TRX token market through "wash trading" via several companies he controls—the TRON Foundation, BitTorrent Foundation Ltd., and Rainberry Inc.—deceiving investors. It is worth noting that the settlement is not without controversy. Amanda Fischer, Director of Policy and Chief Operating Officer of financial reform firm Better Markets, strongly criticized the outcome. Fischer, who previously served as chief of staff to former SEC Chairman Gensler, stated: "Despite the substantial evidence the SEC possesses against Justin Sun and his crypto business, the Commission reached a favorable settlement today. Given the scale and audacity of the alleged fraud against Sun, this is merely a face-saving measure." Fischer urged the presiding judge to reject the settlement and recommended that Congress conduct oversight and review of the SEC's decision.
